school: First prophet and first human being in Islamic cosmology; taught the names of all things; recipient of divine revelation; ancestor of all humanity

books.title: Qisas al-Anbiya (Ibn Kathir)
books.note: Primary source for Adam's story in Islamic tradition

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Baqara (2:30–39)
books.note: The creation and descent of Adam

school: Son of Adam; first prophet appointed after Adam's death in Islamic tradition; received divine scrolls (suhuf) according to hadith. Al-Tabari records him as the guardian of Adam's knowledge. His lineage runs through Idris to Nuh.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adam and Idris in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adam and Idris in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adam and Idris in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adam and Idris in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Quranic prophet raised to a high station; associated in Islamic tradition with the origins of writing, astronomy, and crafts; praised in the Quran as truthful and a prophet raised to a high place

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:56–57)
books.note: Quranic mention of Idris raised to a lofty station

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Idris and Nuh in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Idris and Nuh in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); preached for 950 years; builder of the Ark; ancestor of post-flood humanity; an entire Quranic sura bears his name

books.title: Quran — Sura Nuh (71)
books.note: The full sura dedicated to Nuh's mission

school: Eldest son of Nuh who boarded the Ark; forefather of the Semitic peoples. Al-Tabari traces the full chain of Semitic patriarchs from Sam through to Ibrahim. Ibrahim, Ismail, and Prophet Muhammad all descend through Sam.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Father of Ibrahim; the only father of a prophet named directly in the Quran (6:74). An idol-worshipper whom Ibrahim tried to guide to monotheism; his refusal became a defining trial of Ibrahim's mission. Mentioned four times in the Quran.

books.title: Quran — Sura al-An'am (6:74)
books.note: Ibrahim's address to his father Azar about idol worship

school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); father of monotheism; builder of the Ka'ba with Ismail; progenitor of both the Arab (through Ismail) and Israelite (through Ishaq) prophetic lines; model of complete submission to God

books.title: Quran — Sura Ibrahim (14)
books.note: The sura named for Ibrahim, containing his supplications

school: First-born son of Ibrahim; helped build the Ka'ba in Mecca; ancestor of the Arab peoples and of Prophet Muhammad; known as Dhabih Allah for his willingness to be sacrificed; a prophet sent to the people of ancient Arabia

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Saffat (37:101–113)
books.note: The account of the sacrifice of Ismail

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Ismail and Adnan in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: The furthest point in Muhammad's lineage that classical Muslim scholars consider historically reliable. Ibn Ishaq explicitly instructed scholars not to go beyond Adnan in claiming certainty, as the chain between Ismail and Adnan spans many unverifiable generations. Universally accepted ancestor of the northern (Adnanite) Arab tribes.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: The ancestor after whom the Quraysh tribe is named in the dominant genealogical tradition. The Quraysh were the guardians of the Ka'ba and the noblest tribe in Arabia. All early caliphs and the Prophet himself descended from Fihr. The name 'Quraysh' derives from the Arabic root meaning to gather or concentrate — a reference to Fihr's role consolidating his people.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Great-grandfather of Prophet Muhammad and founder of the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh. He established the two great Meccan trade caravans — the winter journey to Yemen and summer journey to Syria — referenced directly in Sura Quraysh (106). He fed pilgrims during the Hajj, a role so celebrated that his name Hashim means 'the one who crumbles bread'. He died in Gaza during a trading journey to Syria.

books.title: Quran — Sura Quraysh (106)
books.note: The winter and summer caravans established by Hashim ibn Abd Manaf

school: Father of Prophet Muhammad. He married Amina bint Wahb of the Banu Zuhra. He died in Medina during or returning from a trading journey to Syria, either shortly before or just after Muhammad's birth, making the Prophet an orphan from the start. The Quran references this: 'Did He not find you an orphan and shelter you?' (93:6).

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Duha (93:6)
books.note: God's reminder to Muhammad of his orphanhood

school: Sent to the 'Ad people of ancient Arabia; preached monotheism against idol worship; his people were destroyed by a violent wind after rejecting him; a Quranic sura bears his name

books.title: Quran — Sura Hud (11)
books.note: Contains the full account of Hud and the 'Ad

school: Sent to the Thamud people who carved their homes from rock at Al-Hijr (modern Mada'in Salih in Saudi Arabia); the miraculous she-camel was his sign; his people were destroyed by a thunderbolt after killing the camel

books.title: Quran — Sura al-A'raf (7:73–79)
books.note: The story of Salih and Thamud

school: Nephew of Ibrahim; sent to the people of Sodom (Qawm Lut); preached against their moral corruption; his people were destroyed; one of the most frequently mentioned prophets in the Quran

books.title: Quran — Sura Hud (11:77–83)
books.note: The full account of Lut and the destruction of his people

school: Son of Ibrahim and Sarah; father of Yaqub and grandfather of Yusuf; progenitor of the Israelite prophetic line; mentioned 17 times in the Quran; blessed by God as a prophet from birth

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:72)
books.note: Mention of Ishaq as a gift to Ibrahim

school: Son of Ishaq, grandson of Ibrahim; given the name Isra'il; father of the twelve sons who became the progenitors of the twelve tribes of Israel, including Yusuf; prominent in the account of Yusuf in Sura Yusuf

books.title: Quran — Sura Yusuf (12)
books.note: Yaqub features prominently in the story of his son Yusuf

school: Son of Yaqub; his life story is described in the Quran as 'the most beautiful of stories'; sold into slavery by his brothers, rose to become Vizier of Egypt; known for his beauty, patience, wisdom, and ability to interpret dreams; a model of forgiveness

books.title: Quran — Sura Yusuf (12)
books.note: The only Quranic sura dedicated entirely to one prophet's story

school: Prophet sent to the people of Midian; preached against dishonest trade, fraud in weights and measures, and idol worship; considered by some traditions to be the father-in-law of Musa; his people were destroyed when they rejected him

books.title: Quran — Sura al-A'raf (7:85–93)
books.note: The mission of Shu'ayb to the people of Midian

school: Quranic prophet celebrated above all for his extraordinary patience through severe affliction; after years of suffering he called upon God and was healed and restored; his story is the supreme Quranic model of patient endurance (sabr) and trust in God

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:83–84)
books.note: Ayyub's supplication and restoration

school: Mentioned twice in the Quran alongside Ismail and Idris as being among the patient and righteous; identified by some scholars with the biblical Ezekiel; his name (Dhul-Kifl, 'man of the pledge') may refer to a covenant he fulfilled

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:85–86)
books.note: Brief Quranic mention of Dhul-Kifl among the patient

school: Elder brother of Musa; appointed by God as a prophet and minister (wazir) to assist Musa; known for his eloquence; became the first High Priest of the Israelites; his story is inseparable from that of Musa in the Quranic narrative

books.title: Quran — Sura Ta-Ha (20:29–36)
books.note: Musa's prayer to God to appoint Harun as his helper

school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); most frequently mentioned prophet in the Quran; led the Israelites out of Egypt; received the Torah on Mount Sinai; spoke directly with God without intermediary; the supreme model of divine communication and social liberation

books.title: Quran — Sura Ta-Ha (20)
books.note: A complete retelling of Musa's mission

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Qasas (28)
books.note: The story of Musa from birth through his mission

school: Prophet-King of Israel; recipient of the Zabur (Psalms); given wisdom and sovereignty; slew the giant Jalut (Goliath) as a young man; his voice in glorification of God caused mountains and birds to join him; father of Sulayman; established Jerusalem as the holy city

books.title: Quran — Sura Saba' (34:10–11)
books.note: God's gifts to Dawud: softening iron and the glorification of mountains

school: Son of Dawud; given unparalleled dominion over humans, jinn, animals, and the wind; builder of the Temple in Jerusalem; known for his extraordinary wisdom; the Queen of Sheba (Bilqis) converted under his influence; a whole sura of the Quran is named for the ants (al-Naml) in his story

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Naml (27:15–44)
books.note: The story of Sulayman, the hoopoe, and the Queen of Sheba

school: Prophet of the northern Israelite kingdom; fought against idol worship (Baal) at Mount Carmel; praised in the Quran as among the righteous; his identity with the mysterious figure of Khidr is debated in Islamic tradition; known for miracles and fiery zeal for monotheism

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Saffat (37:123–132)
books.note: The Quranic account of Ilyas among the messengers

school: Prophet of the Israelite kingdom; disciple and spiritual successor of Ilyas; mentioned twice in the Quran as one whom God chose above all others; known in biblical tradition for extraordinary miracles including reviving the dead; carried on Ilyas's mission of monotheism

books.title: Quran — Sura al-An'am (6:86)
books.note: Al-Yasa' listed among the prophets guided by God

school: Prophet sent to Nineveh; left his mission prematurely and was swallowed by a great fish; called upon God from within darkness with the prayer of Dhul-Nun (Tasbih Yunus); his supplication from the belly of the whale is among the most powerful prayers in the Quran; his people repented en masse

books.title: Quran — Sura Yunus (10)
books.note: The sura named for Yunus

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:87–88)
books.note: Yunus's prayer from within the whale: La ilaha illa Anta

school: Temple priest and prophet in Jerusalem; guardian of Maryam (Mary); prayed for a son in old age and was miraculously granted Yahya (John the Baptist); his story in the Quran is told in Sura Maryam; a model of persisting in prayer despite old age and apparent impossibility

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:2–11)
books.note: The prayer of Zakariyya and the announcement of Yahya

school: Son of Zakariyya; given wisdom as a child; described in the Quran as Sayyid (chief/master), Hasur (chaste/restrained), and a prophet; contemporary of Isa; martyred by beheading; a supreme model of purity, compassion, and asceticism in Islamic tradition

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:12–15)
books.note: God's gifts to Yahya: wisdom, compassion, and purity

school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); born miraculously of the Virgin Maryam; given the Injil (Gospel); performed miracles by God's leave including healing the blind and raising the dead; in Islam he was neither crucified nor killed but raised by God; will return before the Day of Judgment

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:16–40)
books.note: The birth of Isa and his first miracle of speaking as an infant

books.title: Quran — Sura Al 'Imran (3:45–55)
books.note: The annunciation and the miracles of Isa

school: Prophetic Foundation — Source of All Sufi Chains

books.title: The Quran (Divine Revelation, not personally authored)
books.note: Not a personal work. Free translations at quran.com

parents: 'Abdullah ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib / Amina bint Wahb b 'Abd Munaf bin Zuhrah
children: al-Qasim bin Muhammad , Zaynab bint Muhammad , Ruqayyah bint Muhammad , Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad , Fatima bint Muhammad , 'Abdullah bin Muhammad , Ibrahim bin Muhammad
spouses: Khadijah , Sawda bint Zam'a , 'Aisha bint Abi Bakr , Hafsa bint Umar , Zaynab bint Khazeemah , Umm Salamah , Zaynab bint Jahsh , Juwayriya bint al-Harith , Umm Habibah bint Abi Sufyan , Safiyya bint Huyayy , Maymuna bint al-Harith , Maria al-Qibtiyya

school: Al-Qarawiyyin — Founded 859 CE in Fez; fasted throughout 2-year construction; produced Ibn Rushd, Ibn Khaldun, Maimonides
Baghdad School / Qadiriyya Order — Founder

books.title: Futuh al-Ghayb (Revelations of the Unseen)
books.note: 62 discourses on Sufi spiritual discipline — English

books.title: The Secret of Secrets (Sir al-Asrar)
books.note: Sufi sermons and spiritual guidance — English

school: Yeseviyya Order — Founder / Central Asian Turkic School

books.title: Diwan-i Hikmet (Book of Wisdom-Poetry)
books.note: First great Turkic Sufi poetry collection.

school: Rifaiyya Order — founder of the oldest surviving Sufi order; called the 'Sultan of the Sufi Orders'

books.title: Al-Hikam al-Rifaiyya
books.url: ,note:"Collected wisdom and teachings of the Rifai path

school: Chishti Order — Founder in Indian Subcontinent
Suhrawardiyya Order — Founder (distinct from the Illuminationist philosopher)

books.title: Awarif al-Ma'arif (Gifts of Deep Knowledge)
books.note: Most widely used Sufi manual in the Eastern Islamic world for centuries.

school: Kubrawiyya Order — Founder. Died defending Urgench against the Mongols 1221.

books.title: Fawa'ih al-Jamal wa Fawatih al-Jalal (Fragrances of Beauty, Openings of Majesty)
books.note: Pioneering work on psychic experiences and coloured lights in mystical states.

school: Shadhiliyya Order — Founder

books.title: Hizb al-Bahr (Litany of the Sea)
books.note: One of the most recited prayers in the Islamic world; said to have been dictated by the Prophet in vision.

school: Badawiyya Order — Founder. The most popular Egyptian saint; his mawlid in Tanta is the largest religious festival in Egypt

books.title: Oral teachings (no extant text)
books.note: His annual mawlid in Tanta attracts millions; the Badawiyya is one of the four major Egyptian Sufi orders.

school: Mawlawiyya Order — Founder / Konya School

books.title: Masnavi-ye Manavi (Spiritual Couplets) — ~25,000 verses
books.note: Jami called it 'The Quran in the Persian language.' Greatest mystical epic in any language.

books.title: Fihi Ma Fihi (In It What Is In It — Discourses)
The Mathnawi (Nicholson translation)
books.note: Definitive 6-book Persian Sufi epic — English

books.title: Masnavi (full Persian & English bilingual)
books.note: Full bilingual edition

school: Bektashi Order — founded the Alevi-Bektashi tradition; patron of the Janissaries
Mawlawiyya — Rumi's son who organised the Mawlawi order after his father's death; wrote the first biography of Rumi

books.title: Ibtidaname
books.url: ,note:"First biography of Rumi — essential source on the Mawlawi tradition's founding

school: Burhaniyya Order — Founder. One of the four poles of Egyptian Sufism. The order spread to Sudan and North Africa.

books.title: Oral teachings and prayers
books.note: The Burhaniyya-Muhammadiyya order headquartered in Egypt has spread across North Africa and Sudan.

school: Naqshbandiyya Order — Founder

books.title: The Eleven Principles of the Naqshbandiyya (oral — no extant book)
books.note: His teachings were transmitted orally; the Eleven Principles are his core legacy.

school: Ni'matullahi order founder — founded a major Sufi order that survives today in Iran and South Asia
Hurufiyya — Founder; brought Ibn Arabi's letter mysticism to its apocalyptic extreme; influenced Bektashi order and Imadaddin Nasimi
Javanese Sufism — the nine saints who peacefully Islamized Java through culture, music (gamelan) and mysticism in the 15th century

books.title: Suluk literature
books.url: ,note:"Mystical poetry and teachings attributed to the Nine Saints

books.title: Al-Irshad
books.url: ,note:"Major work on the lives of the Twelve Imams

school: Wali Songo — chief architect of the Demak Sultanate, the first Islamic kingdom in Java; established the Ampel Mosque in Surabaya which became the centre of Islamic learning in Java; mentor to Sunan Kalijaga and Sunan Gunung Jati

books.title: Teachings preserved in Javanese Islamic tradition

school: Wali Songo — founded the Sultanate of Cirebon and Banten; studied in Mecca and Egypt before returning to Islamise West Java; established the political infrastructure of Islamic governance in Sunda
Wali Songo — used Javanese shadow puppetry (Wayang Kulit) and gamelan music to transmit Islamic ethics; brought Sufism to the Javanese cultural heartland; student of Sunan Bonang
Celvetiyye Order founder — founded a major Ottoman Sufi order; teacher of Sultan Ahmed I; buried in Üsküdar
Tijaniyya Order founder — the most widespread Sufi order in sub-Saharan Africa today; claimed direct vision of the Prophet

books.title: Jawahir al-Ma'ani
books.url: ,note:"Foundational text of the Tijaniyya compiled by his disciple Ali Harazim

school: Shadhiliyya lineage — from Fes he travelled to Mecca, then Yemen, teaching a synthesis of rigorous Prophetic practice and deep Sufi love; his disciples founded the Sanusiyya (Libya/Chad), Rashidiyya, Mirghaniyya (Sudan/Horn of Africa) and Khatmiyya — shaping the religion of millions across Africa to this day

books.title: Salat al-Fatih (The Opening Prayer)
books.note: The Salat al-Fatih became one of the most widely recited prayers in Sufi Africa — attributed by his disciples to divine inspiration.

school: Sanusiyya Order — founded in Libya; led the resistance against Italian colonialism; deeply influenced modern Libya

books.title: Bughyat al-Maqasid
books.url: ,note:"Summary of Islamic spiritual path — core Sanusiyya text

books.title: Asrar al-Arifin
books.url: ,note:"First major work of Islamic mysticism in the Malay language

school: Shadhiliyya-'Alawiyya Order — Founder
Chishti / Universal Sufism — Founder (Western Sufism)

books.title: The Mysticism of Sound and Music
books.note: Introduces Sufi teachings on music, sound and vibration to Western audiences.
